topics.title: Performance Patterns — Circuit Breaker, Retry, Bulkhead & Idempotency
topics.description: Resilience patterns prevent cascading failures in distributed systems. Circuit Breaker stops calls to a failing service, allowing it to recover. Retry handles transient failures with exponential backoff and jitter. Bulkhead isolates failures to a subset of resources (like ship compartments). Idempotency ensures duplicate requests (network retries) don't cause double-processing. Together these patterns define 'fault tolerance engineering' — critical for any production microservices interview.
topics.story: Without these patterns, one failing downstream service can bring down your entire application. Circuit breaker is an electrical fuse: too much current (failures) flips the breaker, protecting the rest of the circuit. Retry with exponential backoff is like redialing after a busy signal — wait a bit before trying again, wait longer each time so you don't overwhelm the already-struggling service. Idempotency is a hotel key card: swiping it 5 times still opens the door once — no 5 rooms charged.
topics.analogy: Circuit Breaker: CLOSED=normal, OPEN=stop calling, HALF_OPEN=try one probe. Retry: attempt 1 → wait 1s → attempt 2 → wait 2s → attempt 3 → wait 4s + jitter. Exponential backoff: waitTime = min(base * 2^attempt, maxWait). Jitter: add random(0, waitTime) to prevent thundering herd (all retrying at same time). Bulkhead: separate thread pools per downstream service — if service A hangs, its pool exhausts but service B's pool is unaffected. Idempotency key: client sends UUID with request; server deduplicates using that UUID.
topics.memoryAnchor: Circuit breaker: failureRateThreshold (%), slidingWindowSize, waitDurationInOpenState. Retry: maxAttempts, waitDuration, exponentialBackoffMultiplier. Jitter: random delay to spread retries. Bulkhead: maxConcurrentCalls (thread pool), maxWaitDuration (queue). Timeout: always set — never call without timeout. Idempotency: store (idempotency_key, result) in DB/cache. Return cached result for duplicate. TTL: 24h-7d. Saga: distributed transaction with compensation. Outbox pattern: write event to same DB as state change (atomicity), relay from outbox to message queue.
topics.firstPrinciples: Cascading failure: service A calls B, B is slow → A's threads queue up waiting → A's thread pool exhausts → A becomes slow → services calling A also pile up. Circuit breaker breaks this chain by failing fast. Thundering herd: N services retry simultaneously after a failure → N requests arrive at once when the downstream recovers → overwhelms it again. Jitter spreads retry times across a window, avoiding synchronized retry storms. Idempotency is required whenever you use retry — without it, retry = duplicate processing.
topics.bruteForce: No circuit breaker: threads block on slow service until thread pool exhausted → full system down. No retry: transient network blip = user error. Retry without backoff: thundering herd amplifies the failure. Retry without idempotency: user charged twice for same order.
topics.optimizedApproach: Resilience4j: @CircuitBreaker + @Retry + @Bulkhead + @TimeLimiter as annotations. Exponential backoff with jitter: base=500ms, max=30s, jitter=±50%. Idempotency key pattern: UUID in request header, server checks idempotency_requests table before processing. Outbox pattern: atomically write to DB + outbox table, relay process publishes events. Dead letter queue for permanently failed messages.
topics.whenToUse: Circuit breaker: all synchronous service-to-service calls. Retry: idempotent operations, transient errors (503, 429, network timeout). Do NOT retry: non-idempotent without idempotency key, 4xx errors (bad request won't succeed on retry), fast-fail scenarios. Bulkhead: when one integration shouldn't affect others. Idempotency: payments, order creation, any state-changing operation the client might retry. Timeout: always — set connect timeout and read timeout.

examples.title: Exponential Backoff with Jitter & Timeout
examples.explanation: Exponential backoff doubles the delay each attempt: 100ms, 200ms, 400ms... Full jitter randomizes the delay between 0 and the calculated max — this is empirically better than other jitter strategies for spreading load (AWS: 'Exponential Backoff and Jitter' blog post). The timeout wrapper uses Future.get(timeout) to fail fast if the operation hangs. Always cancel the future on timeout to free threads.
examples.realWorldUse: AWS SDK, Google Cloud client libraries use exponential backoff with jitter by default. Spring @Retryable: @Retryable(maxAttempts=3, backoff=@Backoff(delay=500, multiplier=2)). DevLearner code execution: retry up to 3 times with 200ms base delay if sandbox returns 503. Never retry 4xx errors — the request is malformed, retrying won't help.

examples.title: Idempotency Key Pattern
examples.explanation: The client generates a UUID before the first attempt and includes it in every retry of the same logical operation. The server stores (key → result) atomically with processing. On duplicate: return the original result without reprocessing. TTL (24h-7d) controls how long idempotency is guaranteed. The DB PRIMARY KEY on idempotency_key provides atomic deduplication — no race condition from concurrent retries.
examples.realWorldUse: Stripe, PayPal, Braintree all require Idempotency-Key header for payment APIs. Exactly-once semantics in Kafka (enable.idempotence=true + transactional.id). AWS SQS standard queues: at-least-once delivery — consumer must be idempotent. DevLearner: submission endpoint — same code submitted twice should not create two submissions. Use (userId, problemId, codeHash) as natural idempotency key.

problems.title: Implement Retry with Exponential Backoff
problems.description: Implement `retry(Runnable action, int maxAttempts, long baseDelayMs)` that retries a failing action with exponential backoff. The delay doubles each attempt. Throw RuntimeException if all attempts fail.
problems.hint1: Loop from 1 to maxAttempts. On exception, calculate delay = baseDelay * 2^(attempt-1).
problems.hint2: Thread.sleep(delay) between retries. Don't sleep after the last failed attempt.
problems.hint3: Catch Exception, rethrow if last attempt. Wrap in RuntimeException if needed.

problems.title: Idempotent Counter Increment
problems.description: Implement an idempotent `increment(String requestId, String counterId)` method. The same requestId must increment the counter only once even if called multiple times. Use an in-memory set to track processed requestIds.
problems.hint1: Maintain a Set<String> of processed requestIds. Check before incrementing.
problems.hint2: Map<String, Integer> for counter values. If requestId already in processedIds, skip increment.
problems.hint3: Return current counter value in both cases (idempotent response).

problems.title: Bulkhead Pattern — Thread Pool Isolation
problems.description: Implement a Bulkhead that limits concurrent calls to N. Any call that cannot acquire a permit immediately should fail fast with BulkheadFullException rather than queuing. Use Semaphore with tryAcquire(0).
problems.hint1: Semaphore(N) with tryAcquire() (no timeout) returns false immediately if no permits available.
problems.hint2: If tryAcquire returns false: throw BulkheadFullException (fail fast, don't wait).
problems.hint3: Always release() in finally block after the action completes.
problems.testCases: 2 tasks succeed, 2 tasks rejected with BulkheadFullException
